response:
Mode: create. Readiness: Stage 2 (model-grounded). Eligibility: eligible. The seller controls one common daily display set; the set is chosen before visitor preferences realize using estimated choice probabilities, margins, and an eight-item limit; visitor choice follows from the eligible set; the cardinality constraint is preserved; and the objective remains expected displayed-assortment margin. All six gates pass because neither ranking nor personalization is introduced.

Business framing: Once per day, an online seller chooses up to eight products to display above the fold. The managerial question is which common set best uses scarce display eligibility to generate expected margin. Displaying a product makes it available in the modeled choice set, from which visitors purchase or exit. Including one product can displace another, so the seller trades the margin and choice appeal of alternative combinations under a fixed eight-product limit. A bounded research question is: Which daily display set of at most eight products maximizes expected margin under estimated visitor-choice probabilities? Scholarly novelty remains provisional because no literature is supplied.

Model mapping: displayed products = binary/common daily decisions; margins and estimated probabilities = decision-time parameters; visitor preferences and realized choices = later uncertainty/outcomes; eight products = cardinality constraint; expected margin = objective. The capacity unit is a display position. Position order, visitor-specific information, inventory, and fulfillment are outside the model; the exact day boundary and monetary units are unspecified.

Evidence and boundaries: 'eight items fit above the fold' is a simplifying assumption. No evidence establishes the quality or stability of choice estimates, realized margin gains, or empirical importance. The model does not optimize rank, position effects, or a visitor-adaptive display. Counterconditions include material position effects or heterogeneous visitors requiring personalization. Primary remaining weakness: choice-model calibration is unsupported.
